an4.178:1.1.eng: “Mendicants, these four individuals are found in the world.

an4.178:1.3.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu aññataraṁ santaṁ cetovimuttiṁ upasampajja viharati.
an4.178:1.3.eng: Take a mendicant who enters and remains in a peaceful release of the heart.

an4.178:1.4.eng: They focus on the cessation of substantial reality,

an4.178:1.5.pli: Tassa sakkāyanirodhaṁ manasi karoto sakkāyanirodhe cittaṁ na pakkhandati nappasīdati na santiṭṭhati nādhimuccati.
an4.178:1.5.eng: but on that their mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided.

an4.178:1.6.pli: Tassa kho evaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno na sakkāyanirodho pāṭikaṅkho.
an4.178:1.6.eng: You wouldn’t expect that mendicant to realize the cessation of substantial reality.

an4.178:1.7.pli: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, puriso lepagatena hatthena sākhaṁ gaṇheyya, tassa so hattho sajjeyyapi gaṇheyyapi bajjheyyapi;
an4.178:1.7.eng: Suppose a person were to grab a branch with a glue-smeared hand. Their hand would stick, hold, and bind to it.

an4.178:1.8.pli: evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu aññataraṁ santaṁ cetovimuttiṁ upasampajja viharati.
an4.178:1.8.eng: In the same way, take a mendicant who enters and remains in a peaceful release of the heart.

an4.178:1.9.eng: They focus on the cessation of substantial reality,

an4.178:1.10.pli: Tassa sakkāyanirodhaṁ manasi karoto sakkāyanirodhe cittaṁ na pakkhandati nappasīdati na santiṭṭhati nādhimuccati.
an4.178:1.10.eng: but on that their mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided.

an4.178:1.11.pli: Tassa kho evaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno na sakkāyanirodho pāṭikaṅkho.
an4.178:1.11.eng: You wouldn’t expect that mendicant to realize the cessation of substantial reality.

an4.178:2.1.pli: Idha pana, bhikkhave, bhikkhu aññataraṁ santaṁ cetovimuttiṁ upasampajja viharati.
an4.178:2.1.eng: Next, take a mendicant who enters and remains in a peaceful release of the heart.

an4.178:2.2.eng: They focus on the cessation of substantial reality,

an4.178:2.3.pli: Tassa sakkāyanirodhaṁ manasi karoto sakkāyanirodhe cittaṁ pakkhandati pasīdati santiṭṭhati adhimuccati.
an4.178:2.3.eng: and on that their mind leaps forth, gains confidence, settles down, and becomes decided.

an4.178:2.4.pli: Tassa kho evaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno sakkāyanirodho pāṭikaṅkho.
an4.178:2.4.eng: You would expect that mendicant to realize the cessation of substantial reality.

an4.178:2.5.pli: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, puriso suddhena hatthena sākhaṁ gaṇheyya, tassa so hattho neva sajjeyya na gaṇheyya na bajjheyya;
an4.178:2.5.eng: Suppose a person were to grab a branch with a clean hand. Their hand wouldn’t stick, hold, or bind to it.

an4.178:2.6.pli: evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu aññataraṁ santaṁ cetovimuttiṁ upasampajja viharati.
an4.178:2.6.eng: In the same way, take a mendicant who enters and remains in a peaceful release of the heart.

an4.178:2.7.eng: They focus on the cessation of substantial reality,

an4.178:2.8.pli: Tassa sakkāyanirodhaṁ manasi karoto sakkāyanirodhe cittaṁ pakkhandati pasīdati santiṭṭhati adhimuccati.
an4.178:2.8.eng: and on that their mind leaps forth, gains confidence, settles down, and becomes decided.

an4.178:2.9.pli: Tassa kho evaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno sakkāyanirodho pāṭikaṅkho.
an4.178:2.9.eng: You would expect that mendicant to realize the cessation of substantial reality.

an4.178:3.1.pli: Idha pana, bhikkhave, bhikkhu aññataraṁ santaṁ cetovimuttiṁ upasampajja viharati.
an4.178:3.1.eng: Next, take a mendicant who enters and remains in a peaceful release of the heart.

an4.178:3.3.pli: Tassa avijjāppabhedaṁ manasi karoto avijjāppabhede cittaṁ na pakkhandati nappasīdati na santiṭṭhati nādhimuccati.
an4.178:3.3.eng: but on that their mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided.

an4.178:3.4.pli: Tassa kho evaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno na avijjāppabhedo pāṭikaṅkho.
an4.178:3.4.eng: You wouldn’t expect that mendicant to smash ignorance.

an4.178:3.5.eng: Suppose there was a billabong that had been stagnant for many years.

an4.178:3.6.pli: Tassā puriso yāni ceva āyamukhāni tāni pidaheyya, yāni ca apāyamukhāni tāni vivareyya, devo ca na sammā dhāraṁ anuppaveccheyya.
an4.178:3.6.eng: And someone was to close off the inlets and open up the drains, and the heavens didn’t provide enough rain.

an4.178:3.7.pli: Evañhi tassā, bhikkhave, jambāliyā na āḷippabhedo pāṭikaṅkho.
an4.178:3.7.eng: You wouldn’t expect that billabong to break its banks.

an4.178:3.8.pli: Evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu aññataraṁ santaṁ cetovimuttiṁ upasampajja viharati.
an4.178:3.8.eng: In the same way, take a mendicant who enters and remains in a certain peaceful release of the heart.

an4.178:3.10.pli: Tassa avijjāppabhedaṁ manasi karoto avijjāppabhede cittaṁ na pakkhandati nappasīdati na santiṭṭhati nādhimuccati.
an4.178:3.10.eng: but on that their mind does not leap forth, gain confidence, settle down, and become decided.

an4.178:3.11.pli: Tassa kho evaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno na avijjāppabhedo pāṭikaṅkho.
an4.178:3.11.eng: You wouldn’t expect that mendicant to smash ignorance.

an4.178:4.1.pli: Idha pana, bhikkhave, bhikkhu aññataraṁ santaṁ cetovimuttiṁ upasampajja viharati.
an4.178:4.1.eng: Next, take a mendicant who enters and remains in a peaceful release of the heart.

an4.178:4.3.pli: Tassa avijjāppabhedaṁ manasi karoto avijjāppabhede cittaṁ pakkhandati pasīdati santiṭṭhati adhimuccati.
an4.178:4.3.eng: and on that their mind leaps forth, gains confidence, settles down, and becomes decided.

an4.178:4.4.pli: Tassa kho evaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno avijjāppabhedo pāṭikaṅkho.
an4.178:4.4.eng: You would expect that mendicant to smash ignorance.

an4.178:4.5.eng: Suppose there was a billabong that had been stagnant for many years.

an4.178:4.6.pli: Tassā puriso yāni ceva āyamukhāni tāni vivareyya, yāni ca apāyamukhāni tāni pidaheyya, devo ca sammā dhāraṁ anuppaveccheyya.
an4.178:4.6.eng: And someone was to open up the inlets and close off the drains, and the heavens provided plenty of rain.

an4.178:4.7.pli: Evañhi tassā, bhikkhave, jambāliyā āḷippabhedo pāṭikaṅkho.
an4.178:4.7.eng: You would expect that billabong to break its banks.

an4.178:4.8.pli: Evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu aññataraṁ santaṁ cetovimuttiṁ upasampajja viharati.
an4.178:4.8.eng: In the same way, take a mendicant who enters and remains in a certain peaceful release of the heart.

an4.178:4.10.pli: Tassa avijjāppabhedaṁ manasi karoto avijjāppabhede cittaṁ pakkhandati pasīdati santiṭṭhati adhimuccati.
an4.178:4.10.eng: and on that their mind leaps forth, gains confidence, settles down, and becomes decided.

an4.178:4.11.pli: Tassa kho evaṁ, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno avijjāppabhedo pāṭikaṅkho.
an4.178:4.11.eng: You would expect that mendicant to smash ignorance.

an4.178:4.12.pli: Ime kho, bhikkhave, cattāro puggalā santo saṁvijjamānā lokasmin”ti.
an4.178:4.12.eng: These are the four individuals found in the world.”

an4.179:1.1.pli: Atha kho āyasmā ānando yenāyasmā sāriputto tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā āyasmatā sāriputtena saddhiṁ sammodi.
an4.179:1.1.eng: Then Venerable Ānanda went up to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him.

an4.179:1.2.pli: Sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho āyasmā ānando āyasmantaṁ sāriputtaṁ etadavoca:
an4.179:1.2.eng: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, Ānanda sat down to one side, and said to Sāriputta:

an4.179:1.3.pli: “ko nu kho, āvuso sāriputta, hetu ko paccayo, yena m’idhekacce sattā diṭṭheva dhamme na parinibbāyantī”ti?
an4.179:1.3.eng: “What is the cause, Reverend Sāriputta, what is the reason why some sentient beings are not fully extinguished in this very life?”

an4.179:2.1.pli: “Idhāvuso ānanda, sattā imā hānabhāgiyā saññāti yathābhūtaṁ nappajānanti, imā ṭhitibhāgiyā saññāti yathābhūtaṁ nappajānanti, imā visesabhāgiyā saññāti yathābhūtaṁ nappajānanti, imā nibbedhabhāgiyā saññāti yathābhūtaṁ nappajānanti.
an4.179:2.1.eng: “Reverend Ānanda, it’s because some sentient beings don’t really understand which perceptions make things worse, which keep things steady, which lead to distinction, and which lead to penetration.

an4.179:2.2.pli: Ayaṁ kho, āvuso ānanda, hetu ayaṁ paccayo, yena m’idhekacce sattā diṭṭheva dhamme na parinibbāyantī”ti.
an4.179:2.2.eng: That’s the cause, that’s the reason why some sentient beings are not fully extinguished in this very life.”

an4.179:3.1.pli: “Ko panāvuso sāriputta, hetu ko paccayo, yena m’idhekacce sattā diṭṭheva dhamme parinibbāyantī”ti?
an4.179:3.1.eng: “What is the cause, Reverend Sāriputta, what is the reason why some sentient beings are fully extinguished in this very life?”

an4.179:3.2.pli: “Idhāvuso ānanda, sattā imā hānabhāgiyā saññāti yathābhūtaṁ pajānanti, imā ṭhitibhāgiyā saññāti yathābhūtaṁ pajānanti, imā visesabhāgiyā saññāti yathābhūtaṁ pajānanti, imā nibbedhabhāgiyā saññāti yathābhūtaṁ pajānanti.
an4.179:3.2.eng: “Reverend Ānanda, it’s because some sentient beings truly understand which perceptions make things worse, which keep things steady, which lead to distinction, and which lead to penetration.

an4.179:3.3.pli: Ayaṁ kho, āvuso ānanda, hetu ayaṁ paccayo, yena m’idhekacce sattā diṭṭheva dhamme parinibbāyantī”ti.
an4.179:3.3.eng: That’s the cause, that’s the reason why some sentient beings are fully extinguished in this very life.”

an4.18:1.1.eng: “Mendicants, there are these four ways of making unprejudiced decisions.

an4.18:1.3.pli: Na chandāgatiṁ gacchati, na dosāgatiṁ gacchati, na mohāgatiṁ gacchati, na bhayāgatiṁ gacchati—
an4.18:1.3.eng: Making decisions unprejudiced by favoritism, hostility, stupidity, and cowardice.

an4.18:1.4.eng: These are the four ways of making unprejudiced decisions.

an4.18:2.1.eng: If you don’t act against the teaching

an4.18:2.2.eng: out of favoritism, hostility, cowardice, and stupidity,

an4.18:2.4.eng: like the moon in the waxing fortnight.”

an4.180:1.1.pli: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā bhoganagare viharati ānandacetiye.
an4.180:1.1.eng: At one time the Buddha was staying near the Bhoga City, at the Ānanda Shrine.

an4.180:1.2.eng: There the Buddha addressed the mendicants,

an4.180:1.6.eng: “Mendicants, I will teach you the four great references.

an4.180:1.7.pli: taṁ suṇātha, sādhukaṁ manasi karotha, bhāsissāmī”ti.
an4.180:1.7.eng: Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.”

an4.180:1.8.pli: “Evaṁ, bhante”ti kho te bhikkhū bhagavato paccassosuṁ.

an4.180:2.1.eng: “Mendicants, what are the four great references?

an4.180:2.3.pli: ‘sammukhā metaṁ, āvuso, bhagavato sutaṁ sammukhā paṭiggahitaṁ—
an4.180:2.3.eng: ‘Reverend, I have heard and learned this in the presence of the Buddha:

an4.180:2.4.pli: ayaṁ dhammo, ayaṁ vinayo, idaṁ satthusāsanan’ti.
an4.180:2.4.eng: this is the teaching, this is the training, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’

an4.180:2.5.pli: Tassa, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno bhāsitaṁ neva abhinanditabbaṁ nappaṭikkositabbaṁ.
an4.180:2.5.eng: You should neither approve nor reject that mendicant’s statement.

an4.180:2.6.pli: Anabhinanditvā appaṭikkositvā tāni padabyañjanāni sādhukaṁ uggahetvā sutte otāretabbāni, vinaye sandassetabbāni.
an4.180:2.6.eng: Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should make sure they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training.

an4.180:2.7.pli: Tāni ce sutte otāriyamānāni vinaye sandassiyamānāni na ceva sutte otaranti na vinaye sandissanti, niṭṭhamettha gantabbaṁ:
an4.180:2.7.eng: If they do not fit in the discourse and are not exhibited in the training, you should draw the conclusion:

an4.180:2.8.pli: ‘addhā idaṁ na ceva tassa bhagavato vacanaṁ arahato sammāsambuddhassa;
an4.180:2.8.eng: ‘Clearly this is not the word of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha.

an4.180:2.9.eng: It has been incorrectly memorized by that mendicant.’

an4.180:2.10.eng: And so you should discard it.

an4.180:3.1.pli: Idha pana, bhikkhave, bhikkhu evaṁ vadeyya:

an4.180:3.2.pli: ‘sammukhā metaṁ, āvuso, bhagavato sutaṁ sammukhā paṭiggahitaṁ—
an4.180:3.2.eng: ‘Reverend, I have heard and learned this in the presence of the Buddha:

an4.180:3.3.pli: ayaṁ dhammo, ayaṁ vinayo, idaṁ satthusāsanan’ti.
an4.180:3.3.eng: this is the teaching, this is the training, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’

an4.180:3.4.pli: Tassa, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno bhāsitaṁ neva abhinanditabbaṁ nappaṭikkositabbaṁ.
an4.180:3.4.eng: You should neither approve nor reject that mendicant’s statement.

an4.180:3.5.pli: Anabhinanditvā appaṭikkositvā tāni padabyañjanāni sādhukaṁ uggahetvā sutte otāretabbāni, vinaye sandassetabbāni.
an4.180:3.5.eng: Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should make sure they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training.

an4.180:3.6.pli: Tāni ce sutte otāriyamānāni vinaye sandassiyamānāni sutte ceva otaranti vinaye ca sandissanti, niṭṭhamettha gantabbaṁ:
an4.180:3.6.eng: If they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training, you should draw the conclusion:

an4.180:3.7.pli: ‘addhā idaṁ tassa bhagavato vacanaṁ arahato sammāsambuddhassa;
an4.180:3.7.eng: ‘Clearly this is the word of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha.

an4.180:3.8.eng: It has been correctly memorized by that mendicant.’

an4.180:3.9.eng: You should remember it. This is the first great reference.

an4.180:4.1.pli: Idha pana, bhikkhave, bhikkhu evaṁ vadeyya:

an4.180:4.2.pli: ‘asukasmiṁ nāma āvāse saṅgho viharati sathero sapāmokkho.
an4.180:4.2.eng: ‘In such-and-such monastery lives a Saṅgha with seniors and leaders.

an4.180:4.3.pli: Tassa me saṅghassa sammukhā sutaṁ sammukhā paṭiggahitaṁ—
an4.180:4.3.eng: I’ve heard and learned this in the presence of that Saṅgha:

an4.180:4.4.pli: ayaṁ dhammo, ayaṁ vinayo, idaṁ satthusāsanan’ti.
an4.180:4.4.eng: this is the teaching, this is the training, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’

an4.180:4.5.pli: Tassa, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno bhāsitaṁ neva abhinanditabbaṁ nappaṭikkositabbaṁ.
an4.180:4.5.eng: You should neither approve nor reject that mendicant’s statement.

an4.180:4.6.pli: Anabhinanditvā appaṭikkositvā tāni padabyañjanāni sādhukaṁ uggahetvā sutte otāretabbāni, vinaye sandassetabbāni.
an4.180:4.6.eng: Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should make sure they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training.

an4.180:4.7.pli: Tāni ce sutte otāriyamānāni vinaye sandassiyamānāni na ceva sutte otaranti na vinaye sandissanti, niṭṭhamettha gantabbaṁ:
an4.180:4.7.eng: If they do not fit in the discourse and are not exhibited in the training, you should draw the conclusion:

an4.180:4.8.pli: ‘addhā idaṁ na ceva tassa bhagavato vacanaṁ arahato sammāsambuddhassa;
an4.180:4.8.eng: ‘Clearly this is not the word of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha.

an4.180:4.9.eng: It has been incorrectly memorized by that Saṅgha.’

an4.180:4.10.eng: And so you should discard it.

an4.180:5.1.pli: Idha pana, bhikkhave, bhikkhu evaṁ vadeyya:

an4.180:5.2.pli: ‘asukasmiṁ nāma āvāse saṅgho viharati sathero sapāmokkho.
an4.180:5.2.eng: ‘In such-and-such monastery lives a Saṅgha with seniors and leaders.

an4.180:5.3.pli: Tassa me saṅghassa sammukhā sutaṁ sammukhā paṭiggahitaṁ—
an4.180:5.3.eng: I’ve heard and learned this in the presence of that Saṅgha:

an4.180:5.4.pli: ayaṁ dhammo, ayaṁ vinayo, idaṁ satthusāsanan’ti.
an4.180:5.4.eng: this is the teaching, this is the training, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’

an4.180:5.5.pli: Tassa, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno bhāsitaṁ neva abhinanditabbaṁ nappaṭikkositabbaṁ.
an4.180:5.5.eng: You should neither approve nor reject that mendicant’s statement.

an4.180:5.6.pli: Anabhinanditvā appaṭikkositvā tāni padabyañjanāni sādhukaṁ uggahetvā sutte otāretabbāni, vinaye sandassetabbāni.
an4.180:5.6.eng: Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should make sure they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training.

an4.180:5.7.pli: Tāni ce sutte otāriyamānāni, vinaye sandassiyamānāni sutte ceva otaranti vinaye ca sandissanti, niṭṭhamettha gantabbaṁ:
an4.180:5.7.eng: If they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training, you should draw the conclusion:

an4.180:5.8.pli: ‘addhā idaṁ tassa bhagavato vacanaṁ arahato sammāsambuddhassa;
an4.180:5.8.eng: ‘Clearly this is the word of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha.

an4.180:5.9.eng: It has been correctly memorized by that Saṅgha.’

an4.180:5.10.eng: You should remember it. This is the second great reference.

an4.180:6.1.pli: Idha pana, bhikkhave, bhikkhu evaṁ vadeyya:

an4.180:6.2.pli: ‘asukasmiṁ nāma āvāse sambahulā therā bhikkhū viharanti bahussutā āgatāgamā dhammadharā vinayadharā mātikādharā.
an4.180:6.2.eng: ‘In such-and-such monastery there are several senior mendicants who are very learned, inheritors of the heritage, who remember the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines.

an4.180:6.3.pli: Tesaṁ me therānaṁ sammukhā sutaṁ sammukhā paṭiggahitaṁ—
an4.180:6.3.eng: I’ve heard and learned this in the presence of those senior mendicants:

an4.180:6.4.pli: ayaṁ dhammo, ayaṁ vinayo, idaṁ satthusāsanan’ti.
an4.180:6.4.eng: this is the teaching, this is the training, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’

an4.180:6.5.pli: Tassa, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno bhāsitaṁ neva abhinanditabbaṁ nappaṭikkositabbaṁ.
an4.180:6.5.eng: You should neither approve nor reject that mendicant’s statement.

an4.180:6.6.pli: Anabhinanditvā appaṭikkositvā tāni padabyañjanāni sādhukaṁ uggahetvā sutte otāretabbāni, vinaye sandassetabbāni.
an4.180:6.6.eng: Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should make sure they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training.

an4.180:6.7.pli: Tāni ce sutte otāriyamānāni vinaye sandassiyamānāni na ceva sutte otaranti na vinaye sandissanti, niṭṭhamettha gantabbaṁ:
an4.180:6.7.eng: If they do not fit in the discourse and are not exhibited in the training, you should draw the conclusion:

an4.180:6.8.pli: ‘addhā idaṁ na ceva tassa bhagavato vacanaṁ arahato sammāsambuddhassa;
an4.180:6.8.eng: ‘Clearly this is not the word of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha.

an4.180:6.9.eng: It has been incorrectly memorized by those senior mendicants.’

an4.180:6.10.eng: And so you should discard it.

an4.180:7.1.pli: Idha pana, bhikkhave, bhikkhu evaṁ vadeyya:

an4.180:7.2.pli: ‘asukasmiṁ nāma āvāse sambahulā therā bhikkhū viharanti bahussutā āgatāgamā dhammadharā vinayadharā mātikādharā.
an4.180:7.2.eng: ‘In such-and-such monastery there are several senior mendicants who are very learned, inheritors of the heritage, who remember the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines.

an4.180:7.3.pli: Tesaṁ me therānaṁ sammukhā sutaṁ sammukhā paṭiggahitaṁ—
an4.180:7.3.eng: I’ve heard and learned this in the presence of those senior mendicants:

an4.180:7.4.pli: ayaṁ dhammo, ayaṁ vinayo, idaṁ satthusāsanan’ti.
an4.180:7.4.eng: this is the teaching, this is the monastic law, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’

an4.180:7.5.pli: Tassa, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno bhāsitaṁ neva abhinanditabbaṁ nappaṭikkositabbaṁ.
an4.180:7.5.eng: You should neither approve nor reject that mendicant’s statement.

an4.180:7.6.pli: Anabhinanditvā appaṭikkositvā tāni padabyañjanāni sādhukaṁ uggahetvā sutte otāretabbāni, vinaye sandassetabbāni.
an4.180:7.6.eng: Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should make sure they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training.

an4.180:7.7.pli: Tāni ce sutte otāriyamānāni vinaye sandassiyamānāni sutte ceva otaranti vinaye ca sandissanti, niṭṭhamettha gantabbaṁ:
an4.180:7.7.eng: If they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training, you should draw the conclusion:

an4.180:7.8.pli: ‘addhā idaṁ tassa bhagavato vacanaṁ arahato sammāsambuddhassa;
an4.180:7.8.eng: ‘Clearly this is the word of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha.

an4.180:7.9.eng: It has been correctly memorized by those senior mendicants.’

an4.180:7.10.eng: You should remember it. This is the third great reference.

an4.180:8.1.pli: Idha pana, bhikkhave, bhikkhu evaṁ vadeyya:

an4.180:8.2.pli: ‘asukasmiṁ nāma āvāse eko thero bhikkhu viharati bahussuto āgatāgamo dhammadharo vinayadharo mātikādharo.
an4.180:8.2.eng: ‘In such-and-such monastery there is a single senior mendicant who is very learned, an inheritor of the heritage, who has memorized the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines.

an4.180:8.3.pli: Tassa me therassa sammukhā sutaṁ sammukhā paṭiggahitaṁ—
an4.180:8.3.eng: I’ve heard and learned this in the presence of that senior mendicant:

an4.180:8.4.pli: ayaṁ dhammo, ayaṁ vinayo, idaṁ satthusāsanan’ti.
an4.180:8.4.eng: this is the teaching, this is the training, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’

an4.180:8.5.pli: Tassa, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno bhāsitaṁ neva abhinanditabbaṁ nappaṭikkositabbaṁ.
an4.180:8.5.eng: You should neither approve nor reject that mendicant’s statement.

an4.180:8.6.pli: Anabhinanditvā appaṭikkositvā tāni padabyañjanāni sādhukaṁ uggahetvā sutte otāretabbāni, vinaye sandassetabbāni.
an4.180:8.6.eng: Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should make sure they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training.

an4.180:8.7.pli: Tāni ce sutte otāriyamānāni vinaye sandassiyamānāni na ceva sutte otaranti na vinaye sandissanti, niṭṭhamettha gantabbaṁ:
an4.180:8.7.eng: If they do not fit in the discourse and are not exhibited in the training, you should draw the conclusion:

an4.180:8.8.pli: ‘addhā idaṁ na ceva tassa bhagavato vacanaṁ arahato sammāsambuddhassa;
an4.180:8.8.eng: ‘Clearly this is not the word of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha.

an4.180:8.9.eng: It has been incorrectly memorized by that senior mendicant.’

an4.180:8.10.eng: And so you should discard it.

an4.180:9.1.pli: Idha pana, bhikkhave, bhikkhu evaṁ vadeyya:

an4.180:9.2.pli: ‘asukasmiṁ nāma āvāse eko thero bhikkhu viharati bahussuto āgatāgamo dhammadharo vinayadharo mātikādharo.
an4.180:9.2.eng: ‘In such-and-such monastery there is a single senior mendicant who is very learned, an inheritor of the heritage, who has memorized the teachings, the monastic law, and the outlines.

an4.180:9.3.pli: Tassa me therassa sammukhā sutaṁ sammukhā paṭiggahitaṁ—
an4.180:9.3.eng: I’ve heard and learned this in the presence of that senior mendicant:

an4.180:9.4.pli: ayaṁ dhammo, ayaṁ vinayo, idaṁ satthusāsanan’ti.
an4.180:9.4.eng: this is the teaching, this is the training, this is the Teacher’s instruction.’

an4.180:9.5.pli: Tassa, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno bhāsitaṁ neva abhinanditabbaṁ nappaṭikkositabbaṁ.
an4.180:9.5.eng: You should neither approve nor reject that mendicant’s statement.

an4.180:9.6.pli: Anabhinanditvā appaṭikkositvā tāni padabyañjanāni sādhukaṁ uggahetvā sutte otāretabbāni, vinaye sandassetabbāni.
an4.180:9.6.eng: Instead, having carefully memorized those words and phrases, you should make sure they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training.

an4.180:9.7.pli: Tāni ce sutte otāriyamānāni vinaye sandassiyamānāni sutte ceva otaranti vinaye ca sandissanti, niṭṭhamettha gantabbaṁ:
an4.180:9.7.eng: If they fit in the discourse and are exhibited in the training, you should draw the conclusion:

an4.180:9.8.pli: ‘addhā idaṁ tassa bhagavato vacanaṁ arahato sammāsambuddhassa;
an4.180:9.8.eng: ‘Clearly this is the word of the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha.

an4.180:9.9.eng: It has been correctly memorized by that senior mendicant.’

an4.180:9.10.eng: You should remember it. This is the fourth great reference.

an4.180:9.11.eng: These are the four great references.”

an4.181:1.1.pli: “Catūhi, bhikkhave, aṅgehi samannāgato yodhājīvo rājāraho hoti rājabhoggo, rañño aṅganteva saṅkhaṁ gacchati.
an4.181:1.1.eng: “Mendicants, a warrior with four factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship.

an4.181:1.3.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, yodhājīvo ṭhānakusalo ca hoti, dūrepātī ca, akkhaṇavedhī ca, mahato ca kāyassa padāletā.
an4.181:1.3.eng: He’s skilled in the basics, a long-distance shooter, a marksman, one who shatters large objects.

an4.181:1.4.pli: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, catūhi aṅgehi samannāgato yodhājīvo rājāraho hoti rājabhoggo, rañño aṅganteva saṅkhaṁ gacchati.
an4.181:1.4.eng: A warrior with these four factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and is reckoned a factor of kingship.

an4.181:1.5.pli: Evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, catūhi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu āhuneyyo hoti pāhuneyyo dakkhiṇeyyo añjalikaraṇīyo anuttaraṁ puññakkhettaṁ lokassa.
an4.181:1.5.eng: In the same way, a mendicant with four qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with joined palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.

an4.181:1.7.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu ṭhānakusalo ca hoti, dūrepātī ca, akkhaṇavedhī ca, mahato ca kāyassa padāletā.
an4.181:1.7.eng: He’s skilled in the basics, a long-distance shooter, a marksman, one who shatters large objects.

an4.181:2.1.eng: And how is a mendicant skilled in the basics?

an4.181:2.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sīlavā hoti …pe… samādāya sikkhati sikkhāpadesu.
an4.181:2.2.eng: It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken.

an4.181:2.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu ṭhānakusalo hoti.
an4.181:2.3.eng: That’s how a mendicant is skilled in the basics.

an4.181:3.1.eng: And how is a mendicant a long-distance shooter?

an4.181:3.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu yaṁ kiñci rūpaṁ atītānāgatapaccuppannaṁ ajjhattaṁ vā bahiddhā vā oḷārikaṁ vā sukhumaṁ vā hīnaṁ vā paṇītaṁ vā yaṁ dūre santike vā, sabbaṁ rūpaṁ ‘netaṁ mama, nesohamasmi, na meso attā’ti evametaṁ yathābhūtaṁ sammappaññāya passati.
an4.181:3.2.eng: It’s when a mendicant truly sees any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: all form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’

an4.181:3.3.eng: They truly see any kind of feeling …

an4.181:3.6.pli: yaṁ kiñci viññāṇaṁ atītānāgatapaccuppannaṁ ajjhattaṁ vā bahiddhā vā oḷārikaṁ vā sukhumaṁ vā hīnaṁ vā paṇītaṁ vā yaṁ dūre santike vā, sabbaṁ viññāṇaṁ ‘netaṁ mama, nesohamasmi, na meso attā’ti evametaṁ yathābhūtaṁ sammappaññāya passati.
an4.181:3.6.eng: consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near, all consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’

an4.181:3.7.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu dūrepātī hoti.
an4.181:3.7.eng: That’s how a mendicant is a long-distance shooter.

an4.181:4.1.eng: And how is a mendicant a marksman?

an4.181:4.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu ‘idaṁ dukkhan’ti yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti …pe… ‘ayaṁ dukkhanirodhagāminī paṭipadā’ti yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti.
an4.181:4.2.eng: It’s when they truly understand: ‘This is suffering’ … ‘This is the origin of suffering’ … ‘This is the cessation of suffering’ … ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’.

an4.181:4.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu akkhaṇavedhī hoti.
an4.181:4.3.eng: That’s how a mendicant is a marksman.

an4.181:5.1.pli: Kathañca, bhikkhave, bhikkhu mahato kāyassa padāletā hoti?
an4.181:5.1.eng: And how does a mendicant shatter large objects?

an4.181:5.2.pli: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu mahantaṁ avijjākkhandhaṁ padāletā.
an4.181:5.2.eng: It’s when a mendicant shatters the great mass of ignorance.

an4.181:5.3.pli: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu mahato kāyassa padāletā hoti.
an4.181:5.3.eng: That’s how a mendicant shatters large objects.

an4.181:5.4.pli: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, catūhi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu āhuneyyo hoti …pe… anuttaraṁ puññakkhettaṁ lokassā”ti.
an4.181:5.4.eng: A mendicant with these four qualities … is the supreme field of merit for the world.”

an4.182:1.1.eng: “There are four things that no-one can guarantee—

an4.182:1.2.pli: samaṇo vā brāhmaṇo vā devo vā māro vā brahmā vā koci vā lokasmiṁ.
an4.182:1.2.eng: not an ascetic, a brahmin, a god, a Māra, a divinity, or anyone in the world.

an4.182:2.2.eng: No-one can guarantee that someone liable to old age will not grow old.

an4.182:2.3.pli: samaṇo vā brāhmaṇo vā devo vā māro vā brahmā vā koci vā lokasmiṁ;

an4.182:2.4.eng: No-one can guarantee that someone liable to sickness will not get sick.

an4.182:2.5.pli: samaṇo vā brāhmaṇo vā devo vā māro vā brahmā vā koci vā lokasmiṁ;

an4.182:2.6.eng: No-one can guarantee that someone liable to death will not die.

an4.182:2.7.pli: samaṇo vā brāhmaṇo vā devo vā māro vā brahmā vā koci vā lokasmiṁ;

an4.182:2.8.pli: ‘yāni kho pana tāni pubbe attanā katāni pāpakāni kammāni saṅkilesikāni ponobhavikāni sadarāni dukkhavipākāni āyatiṁ jātijarāmaraṇikāni, tesaṁ vipāko mā nibbattī’ti natthi koci pāṭibhogo—
an4.182:2.8.eng: No-one can guarantee that the bad deeds done in past lives—corrupting, leading to future lives, hurtful, resulting in suffering and future rebirth, old age, and death—will not produce their result.

an4.182:2.9.pli: samaṇo vā brāhmaṇo vā devo vā māro vā brahmā vā koci vā lokasmiṁ.

an4.182:3.1.pli: Imesaṁ kho, bhikkhave, catunnaṁ dhammānaṁ natthi koci pāṭibhogo—
an4.182:3.1.eng: These are the four things that no-one can guarantee—

an4.182:3.2.pli: samaṇo vā brāhmaṇo vā devo vā māro vā brahmā vā koci vā lokasmin”ti.
an4.182:3.2.eng: not an ascetic, a brahmin, a god, a Māra, a divinity, or anyone in the world.”

an4.183:1.1.pli: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā rājagahe viharati veḷuvane kalandakanivāpe.
an4.183:1.1.eng: At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground.

an4.183:1.2.pli: Atha kho vassakāro brāhmaṇo magadhamahāmatto yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavatā saddhiṁ sammodi.
an4.183:1.2.eng: Then Vassakāra the brahmin, a chief minister of Magadha, went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him.

an4.183:1.3.pli: Sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho vassakāro brāhmaṇo magadhamahāmatto bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
an4.183:1.3.eng: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to the Buddha:

an4.183:2.1.eng: “Worthy Gotama, this is my doctrine and view:

an4.183:2.2.eng: There’s nothing wrong with talking about what you’ve seen, saying:

an4.183:2.3.pli: evaṁ me diṭṭhanti, natthi tato doso;

an4.183:2.4.eng: There’s nothing wrong with talking about what you’ve heard, saying:

an4.183:2.5.pli: evaṁ me sutanti, natthi tato doso;

an4.183:2.6.eng: There’s nothing wrong with talking about what you’ve thought, saying:

an4.183:2.7.pli: evaṁ me mutanti, natthi tato doso;

an4.183:2.8.eng: There’s nothing wrong with talking about what you’ve known, saying:

an4.183:2.9.pli: evaṁ me viññātanti, natthi tato doso’”ti.

an4.183:3.1.eng: “Brahmin, I don’t say you should talk about everything you see, hear, think, and know.

an4.183:3.2.pli: na panāhaṁ, brāhmaṇa, sabbaṁ diṭṭhaṁ na bhāsitabbanti vadāmi;
an4.183:3.2.eng: But I also don’t say you should talk about nothing you see, hear, think, and know.

an4.183:3.3.pli: nāhaṁ, brāhmaṇa, sabbaṁ sutaṁ bhāsitabbanti vadāmi;

an4.183:3.4.pli: na panāhaṁ, brāhmaṇa, sabbaṁ sutaṁ na bhāsitabbanti vadāmi;

an4.183:3.5.pli: nāhaṁ, brāhmaṇa, sabbaṁ mutaṁ bhāsitabbanti vadāmi;

an4.183:3.6.pli: na panāhaṁ, brāhmaṇa, sabbaṁ mutaṁ na bhāsitabbanti vadāmi;

an4.183:3.7.pli: nāhaṁ, brāhmaṇa, sabbaṁ viññātaṁ bhāsitabbanti vadāmi;

an4.183:3.8.pli: na panāhaṁ, brāhmaṇa, sabbaṁ viññātaṁ na bhāsitabbanti vadāmi.

an4.183:4.1.pli: Yañhi, brāhmaṇa, diṭṭhaṁ bhāsato akusalā dhammā abhivaḍḍhanti, kusalā dhammā parihāyanti, evarūpaṁ diṭṭhaṁ na bhāsitabbanti vadāmi.
an4.183:4.1.eng: When talking about certain things you’ve seen, heard, thought, or known, unskillful qualities grow while skillful qualities decline. I say that you shouldn’t talk about those things.

an4.183:4.2.pli: Yañca khvassa, brāhmaṇa, diṭṭhaṁ abhāsato kusalā dhammā parihāyanti, akusalā dhammā abhivaḍḍhanti, evarūpaṁ diṭṭhaṁ bhāsitabbanti vadāmi.
an4.183:4.2.eng: When talking about other things you’ve seen, heard, thought, or known, unskillful qualities decline while skillful qualities grow. I say that you should talk about those things.”

an4.183:5.1.pli: Yañhi, brāhmaṇa, sutaṁ bhāsato akusalā dhammā abhivaḍḍhanti, kusalā dhammā parihāyanti, evarūpaṁ sutaṁ na bhāsitabbanti vadāmi.

an4.183:5.2.pli: Yañca khvassa, brāhmaṇa, sutaṁ abhāsato kusalā dhammā parihāyanti, akusalā dhammā abhivaḍḍhanti, evarūpaṁ sutaṁ bhāsitabbanti vadāmi.

an4.183:6.1.pli: Yañhi, brāhmaṇa, mutaṁ bhāsato akusalā dhammā abhivaḍḍhanti, kusalā dhammā parihāyanti, evarūpaṁ mutaṁ na bhāsitabbanti vadāmi.

an4.183:6.2.pli: Yañca khvassa, brāhmaṇa, mutaṁ abhāsato kusalā dhammā parihāyanti, akusalā dhammā abhivaḍḍhanti, evarūpaṁ mutaṁ bhāsitabbanti vadāmi.

an4.183:7.1.pli: Yañhi, brāhmaṇa, viññātaṁ bhāsato akusalā dhammā abhivaḍḍhanti, kusalā dhammā parihāyanti, evarūpaṁ viññātaṁ na bhāsitabbanti vadāmi.

an4.183:7.2.pli: Yañca khvassa, brāhmaṇa, viññātaṁ abhāsato kusalā dhammā parihāyanti, akusalā dhammā abhivaḍḍhanti, evarūpaṁ viññātaṁ bhāsitabbanti vadāmī”ti.

an4.183:8.1.pli: Atha kho vassakāro brāhmaṇo magadhamahāmatto bhagavato bhāsitaṁ abhinanditvā anumoditvā uṭṭhāyāsanā pakkāmīti.
an4.183:8.1.eng: Then Vassakāra the brahmin, having approved and agreed with what the Buddha said, got up from his seat and left.
